content.text: ALZ Terraform Accelerator

The ALZ Terraform accelerator uses the caf-enterprise-scale Terraform module from the Terraform Registry to deploy the full Enterprise-Scale Landing Zone platform. Unlike ALZ-Bicep (where you fork and modify a Bicep repository), the Terraform approach consumes the module as a versioned registry dependency and configures it entirely through input variables. This makes upgrades simpler — bumping the module version number pulls in policy and architecture updates. The module covers management groups, policy definitions, policy assignments, hub networking, management baseline (Log Analytics, Automation), identity, and RBAC.

attrs.alt: ALZ Terraform module architecture with child modules and state management

content.text: The caf-enterprise-scale Terraform module is the official Microsoft-maintained Terraform implementation. It uses the AzureRM provider and requires Terraform >= 1.7 and AzureRM provider ~> 3.100. The module itself is large — it creates hundreds of resources. Always run terraform plan before apply and review the plan carefully, especially for policy assignments.
Root Module Configuration

The root module calls the caf-enterprise-scale registry module with structured input objects for each area: connectivity (hub-spoke or Virtual WAN), management (Log Analytics workspace, Sentinel), identity, and core landing zones (management group hierarchy and policies). All configuration is in HCL input variables — no file forking required. Multiple provider aliases enable cross-subscription deployments from a single Terraform run.
Variable Files per Environment

Different environments (production, development, test) use separate .tfvars files. Sensitive values (subscription IDs, object IDs) are passed via environment variables or a secret manager — never committed to git. The tfvars files override variables defined in variables.tf with environment-specific values. The same Terraform codebase deploys to all environments; only the tfvars file changes.
CI/CD Pipeline for ALZ Terraform

GitHub Actions with OIDC (no client secrets) runs terraform plan on pull requests and posts the plan summary to the PR as a comment. On merge to main, it runs terraform apply with environment protection (manual approval gate). The plan step catches drift, accidental deletions, and policy changes before they reach production. The apply step uses -auto-approve only in the context of the protected environment gate — not in PRs.

# Terraform ALZ deployment pipeline (GitHub Actions with OIDC)
# .github/workflows/alz-terraform.yml (abbreviated)

cat << 'EOF'
name: ALZ Terraform
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths: ['terraform/**']
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
    paths: ['terraform/**']

permissions:
  id-token: write
  contents: read
  pull-requests: write

jobs:
  plan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
        with: { terraform_version: '~> 1.7' }
      - uses: azure/login@v2
        with:
          client-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
          tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
          subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}
      - name: Terraform Init
        run: terraform -chdir=terraform init
      - name: Terraform Validate
        run: terraform -chdir=terraform validate
      - name: Terraform Plan
        run: |
          terraform -chdir=terraform plan \
            -var-file=prod.tfvars \
            -out=tfplan
      - name: Post plan summary to PR
        if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
        run: |
          terraform -chdir=terraform show -no-color tfplan | \
            head -200 >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY

  apply:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: plan
    if: github.event_name == 'push' && github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    environment: production
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
      - uses: azure/login@v2
        with:
          client-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
          tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
          subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}
      - name: Terraform Apply
        run: |
          terraform -chdir=terraform init
          terraform -chdir=terraform apply \
            -var-file=prod.tfvars \
            -auto-approve
EOF
Remote State Bootstrap

Terraform state must be stored in Azure Blob Storage before the ALZ module can run — but the storage account itself can't be managed by the same Terraform config that uses it as a backend (bootstrapping paradox). The solution is to create the storage account and container manually (or with a bootstrap script) before running terraform init. Enable versioning and soft-delete on the state container so accidental state corruption is recoverable. Grant the Terraform service principal Storage Blob Data Contributor on the container (not the full storage account).

# Terraform state management for ALZ
# State lives in Azure Blob Storage with versioning and soft-delete enabled.

# Bootstrap: create the remote state storage account before Terraform can run
SUB_MGMT='<management-sub-id>'
RG='rg-tfstate-mgmt-eastus'
SA='stgtfstatecore'
CONTAINER='tfstate'

az group create --name $RG --location eastus --subscription $SUB_MGMT

az storage account create \
  --name $SA \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --sku Standard_GRS \
  --kind StorageV2 \
  --enable-blob-versioning true \
  --enable-alz-protection true \
  --min-tls-version TLS1_2 \
  --allow-blob-public-access false \
  --subscription $SUB_MGMT

az storage container create \
  --name $CONTAINER \
  --account-name $SA \
  --subscription $SUB_MGMT

# Enable soft delete (30-day retention) on the container
az storage account blob-service-properties update \
  --account-name $SA \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --container-delete-retention-days 30 \
  --enable-container-delete-retention true \
  --subscription $SUB_MGMT

# Grant the Terraform service principal Storage Blob Data Contributor on the container
SA_ID=$(az storage account show --name $SA --resource-group $RG \
  --subscription $SUB_MGMT --query id -o tsv)

az role assignment create \
  --role 'Storage Blob Data Contributor' \
  --assignee-object-id '<terraform-sp-oid>' \
  --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
  --scope "${SA_ID}/blobServices/default/containers/${CONTAINER}"

echo "Remote state backend ready: ${SA}/${CONTAINER}"

# Import existing resources into Terraform state (if bootstrapping over manual infra)
# Example: import an existing management group
terraform import 'module.alz.azurerm_management_group.alz' /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/alz
ALZ Terraform vs ALZ-Bicep Comparison
Factor
ALZ Terraform
ALZ-Bicep
Module consumption
Version pin in module block; upgrade by bumping version
Fork repository; merge upstream changes
State management
Remote state required (Azure Blob); terraform plan shows drift
No state — idempotent ARM deployments; no drift detection between runs
Multi-environment
Separate tfvars files; same code for all envs
Separate parameter files; same Bicep for all envs
Policy updates
Bump module version; run plan to preview policy changes
Merge upstream branch; review CHANGELOG; run what-if
Toolchain fit
Teams already using Terraform for application IaC
Teams using Bicep for other platform resources; Microsoft-first shops
Resource import
terraform import brings existing manual resources under state
ARM deployments are idempotent; no import needed
Complexity
Large Terraform plan with 200+ resources on first apply
Multiple sequential az deployment commands; each is scoped
Summary

The ALZ Terraform accelerator uses the caf-enterprise-scale registry module to deploy the full ESLZ through a root module that calls child modules for management groups, policy, hub networking, management baseline, and identity. Configuration is entirely through input variables and .tfvars files — no module forking required. Remote state lives in Azure Blob Storage with versioning enabled. A GitHub Actions pipeline with OIDC authentication runs plan on PRs and apply on merge, with an environment approval gate protecting production. Choose Terraform when your team already uses it for application IaC; choose ALZ-Bicep for Microsoft-native Bicep workflows.
